capability.name: Source Evaluation
capability.description: Assess credibility, relevance, and reliability of information sources. Distinguish primary from secondary sources, identify bias indicators, evaluate recency and authority. The foundation skill for all research work.

sets.research_topic: the performance impact of React server components vs. client components
sets.source_title: Why Server Components Are 10x Faster — A Benchmark Study
sets.claim: Server components reduce JavaScript bundle size by 87% and improve Time to Interactive by 3.2x compared to equivalent client components.

sets.research_topic: whether TypeScript's type system catches more bugs than unit tests
sets.source_title: An Empirical Study of Type Systems and Software Quality
sets.claim: Projects that adopted TypeScript showed a 15% reduction in production bugs, but no statistically significant difference in bug discovery rate compared to projects with high test coverage.

sets.research_topic: best practices for securing API keys in client-side applications
sets.source_title: Never Put API Keys in Frontend Code — Here's What To Do Instead
sets.source_author: Medium blog post by an anonymous developer
sets.claim: The only secure way to handle API keys in frontend apps is to use a backend proxy. Environment variables, obfuscation, and restricted CORS policies are all insufficient.

criteria.description: Accurately assessed the source's credibility for this specific research context
Correctly classified the source type and its implications

sets.research_topic: optimal database indexing strategy for a high-write workload
sets.source_a_type: AWS blog post by a Principal Engineer
sets.claim_a: For write-heavy workloads, minimize indexes. Each additional index adds 10-20% write latency. Use at most 3-4 indexes per table.
sets.source_b_type: research paper from a small university database lab
sets.claim_b: Partial indexes and write-optimized index structures (LSM trees, BRIN indexes) allow maintaining 8+ indexes with under 5% write overhead in PostgreSQL 15+.

sets.research_topic: whether microservices improve development velocity for small teams
sets.claim_a: Microservices enabled our teams to deploy independently 100+ times per day. The architecture is essential for modern development velocity.
sets.source_b_type: independent survey study of 200 startups (5-20 developers)
sets.claim_b: Teams under 20 developers that adopted microservices saw a median 40% increase in deployment complexity and only 12% improvement in deploy frequency, with 60% reporting they would choose a monolith if starting over.

criteria.description: Showed nuanced reasoning about context-dependent applicability
